content.problemStatement:
Reverse polish notations are easier to parse for computers and you don't need parentheses to denote precedence of operations.


Transform the algebraic expression with brackets into RPN form. 



You can assume that for the test cases below only single letters will be used, brackets [] will not be used and each expression has only one RPN form (no expressions like a*b*c)



Input Format

The first line contains t, the number of test cases (less then 100).
Followed by t lines, containing an expression to be translated to RPN form, where the length of the expression is less then 400.


Output Format
The expressions in RPN form, one per line.
constraints: Only single letters will be used
Each expression has only one RPN form
content.inputFormat: The first line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains a single expression to be translated to RPN form.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the expression in RPN form on a new line.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 1: (a+(b*c))

Start with innermost brackets: b*c becomes bc*.
Replace (b*c) in the original expression: it becomes (a+bc*).
Process the addition: a+bc* becomes abc*+.
Result: abc*+.

Test case 2: ((a+b)*(z+x))

Start with the first set of brackets: a+b becomes ab+.
Process the second set of brackets: z+x becomes zx+.
Combine with the multiplication: (ab+)*(zx+) becomes ab+zx+*.
Result: ab+zx+*.

Test case 3: ((a+t)*((b+(a+c))^(c+d)))

Start with the innermost brackets:

a+c becomes ac+.
b+(a+c) becomes bac++.


Process the next set of brackets: bac++^(c+d):

c+d becomes cd+.
bac++^cd+ combines to bac++cd+^.


Finally, handle the outermost multiplication: (a+t) becomes at+, so (at+)*(bac++cd+^) becomes at+bac++cd+^*.
Result: at+bac++cd+^*

Explanation:
Test case 1: (a+b)

Start with innermost brackets: a+b becomes ab+.
Result: ab+.

Test case 2: (a*b)

Start with innermost brackets: a*b becomes a*b.
Result: a*b.

Explanation:
Test case 1: (a+b*c)

Start with innermost brackets: b*c becomes bc*.
Replace (b*c) in the original expression: it becomes (a+bc*).
Process the addition: a+bc* becomes abc*+.
Result: abc*+.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved by using a stack to keep track of operators. We iterate through the expression and push operators onto the stack when we encounter them. When we encounter a closing parenthesis, we pop operators off the stack and add them to the output until we encounter the corresponding opening parenthesis. We also pop operators off the stack when we encounter an operator with lower precedence than the top of the stack.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N) where T is the number of test cases and N is the maximum length of an expression.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the stack and output string.
